title: Cambridge IGCSE Physics 0625 — Demo Quiz 001
description: A short Quanta Aptus demo quiz using original generated MCQs.
subject: physics

items.skill: Calculate neutron number from nucleon number
items.stem: An atom of silver is represented as having a nucleon number of 108 and a proton number of 47. How many neutrons are in the nucleus of this atom?
items.explanation: The number of neutrons is found by subtracting the proton number from the nucleon number: 108 - 47 = 61.
items.common_misconception: Students often confuse nucleon number with neutron number and give the nucleon number itself as the answer, forgetting that nucleon number = protons + neutrons.

items.skill: Calculate neutron number from nucleon number
items.stem: A nucleus has a proton number of 19 and contains 21 neutrons. What is the nucleon number of this nucleus?
items.explanation: Nucleon number is the total of protons and neutrons: 19 + 21 = 40.
items.common_misconception: Some students subtract the proton number from the neutron number instead of adding them, since they mix up which quantities should be combined.

items.skill: Calculate neutron number from nucleon number
items.stem: Two isotopes of an element X have nucleon numbers 63 and 65. Both isotopes have a proton number of 29. What is the difference in the number of neutrons between these two isotopes?
items.explanation: Neutrons in isotope 1 = 63 - 29 = 34. Neutrons in isotope 2 = 65 - 29 = 36. The difference is 36 - 34 = 2.
items.common_misconception: Students sometimes think isotopes differ in proton number rather than neutron number, leading them to answer 0 because they assume the nuclei are otherwise identical in every count.

items.skill: Calculate neutron number from nucleon number
items.stem: A neutral atom contains 34 electrons and has a nucleon number of 79. How many neutrons does this atom's nucleus contain?
items.explanation: In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons, so the proton number is 34. Neutrons = nucleon number - proton number = 79 - 34 = 45.
items.common_misconception: Students may forget that electron number equals proton number only in a neutral atom, and may instead try to use the electron number directly as a separate quantity unrelated to the proton count.

items.skill: Calculate neutron number from nucleon number
items.stem: A radioactive nucleus with proton number 84 decays by emitting one alpha particle, forming a new nucleus with nucleon number 206. What was the neutron number of the original nucleus before decay?
items.explanation: An alpha particle carries away a nucleon number of 4, so the original nucleus had a nucleon number of 206 + 4 = 210. Its neutron number is therefore 210 - 84 = 126.
items.common_misconception: Students often forget to add back the 4 nucleons lost as the alpha particle before subtracting the proton number, leading them to use the daughter nucleus's nucleon number directly.
